metaTitle: The Boeing Atmosphere Global Documentation Hub - Typography

header.title: Typography
header.description: Typography can help strategically to guide users and establish hierarchy in your product.

sections.text:
Atmosphere strategy

The success of Atmosphere lies in creating a flexible, adaptable system with strong governance and documentation that enables teams to build quickly and efficiently. Atmosphere is managed as a product and is constantly refined based on user data and feedback, making sure that it serves the current needs of DAS and remains future-proof. With a strong focus on modularity, (AI) automation, collaboration, and continuous improvements, Atmosphere will drive both the growth and quality of our digital products while keeping teams aligned and productive.
items: Typography helps establish hierarchy across information in a product
Use a combination of font sizes, weights, and styles to create a visual hierarchy
Consider both font size and text to background contrast for optimal readability
Ensure proper spacing and alignment for intuitive navigation and scannability

sections.text:
Boeing Meso

Boeing has created its own, unique typeface for all digital applications and external marketing materials — Boeing Meso. (It’s pronounced “MEZ-oh,” as in mesosphere.)

Boeing Meso is a contemporary sans-serif typeface which was created for clear legibility in Boeing's digital applications. Carefully adjusted letter spacing and larger x-height make it optimal for reading at smaller sizes. Its wide variety of weights give enough variation to construct a clear type hierarchy.

Type rules

Always follow these standard type rules when working with typography in any of Boeing’s digital applications. Using these type rules strengthens Boeing’s visual identity and helps distinguish it from its competitors in the market.

Weight

Boeing Meso has been created in a variety of weights for maximum flexibility. As a general rule, font combinations work best when the two weights closest to one another are not used together. The Atmosphere design system primarily uses the light, regular, medium and bold weights.

Letter spacing

In general, letter spacing is always set to 0. Smaller text sizes may need more space between each letter for optimised legibility, while larger text may need less space between each letter for optimised legibility.

Tabular figures

Use tabular figures (also known as monospaced numbers) rather than proportional digits in tables, lists or places where values may change often, such as clocks. Tabular numbers keep values optically aligned for better scanning.

Fallback fonts

Boeing Meso is always our default recommended font for web applications. When using it is not possible, or something prevents it from rendering in a specific instance, fallback fonts offer a series of alternatives. Our recommended order is: Boeing Meso, Noto Sans, Helvetica Neue, Arial, system-ui, sans-serif.

Type scale

type scale is a selection of font styles that can be used across an application, ensuring a flexible, yet consistent, style that accommodates a range of purposes.

The Atmosphere type scale is based on the Major Second Musical Scale (1.125), where every size is approximately 12.5% larger than the previous. This scale uses 16px as its base unit size and all sizes are either rounded to prevent decimals or visually adjusted to harmonise with the full scale.

Line height

Line height, also known as leading, controls the amount of space between baselines in a block of text. A text’s line height is proportional to its type size.

The Atmosphere design system primarily uses a set of five different line height scales. These scales should be used in context with the desired type roles. In general, larger type sizes use a smaller scale and smaller type sizes use a larger scale to ensure better readability.

In the Atmosphere type set, line heights were created by multiplying the type sizes with the appropriate line height scale and were adjusted so that the equivalent (px) values were rounded up to the nearest even number.

Letter spacing

Letter spacing, also called tracking, refers to the uniform adjustment of the space between letters in a piece of text.

The Atmosphere design system recommends using letter spacings in context with the chosen type size and weight. Generally larger type sizes with lesser weight require more negative (-) letter spacing, whereas smaller type sizes with more weight require more positive (+) letter spacing to improve readability.

The Atmosphere foundations type set uses five different sets of letter spacing, where negative (-) letter spacings were used in “Display” type role and positive (+) letter spacings were used in type sizes less than or equal to 16px.

Type sets

The Atmosphere type set organizes type styles into five roles that are named to describe their purposes: Display, headline, title, label, and body. These type roles are device-agnostic, allowing them to adapt uniformly at different breakpoints.

Type roles

The Atmosphere type set organizes type styles into five roles that are named to describe their purposes: Display, headline, title, label, and body. These type roles are device-agnostic, allowing them to adapt uniformly at different breakpoints.

Display

The Atmosphere type set uses two display styles: Display-High & Display-Medium. As the largest text on the screen, display styles should be reserved for short, important text or numerals.

Headline

Headlines are best suited for short and more prominent text in the screen. Atmosphere provides three different heading styles in its type set: Heading-High, Heading-Medium , and Heading-Low.

Title

Titles are smaller than headline styles, and should be used for medium prominence text that remains relatively short. For example, consider using title styles to divide secondary passages of text or secondary regions of content. There are three different title styles available in the default type set: Title-High, Title-Medium, and Title-Low.

Body

Body styles are used for longer paragraphs or less prominent text on the screen. There are seven different body styles available in the default type set: Body-High (default), Body-High (highlight), Body-Medium (default), Body-Medium (highlight), Body-Low (default), Body-Low (highlight) and Body-Lowest (default). In general, the Body (default) typestyle should be used as default body text. Body (highlight) should be reserved for special cases like highlighting certain word or phrase inside the text paragraph.

Label

Label styles are smaller, utilitarian styles, used for things like the text inside components or for very small text in the content body, such as captions. Buttons, for example, use the Label-High style. There are five different label styles available in the default type set: Label-High, Label-Medium, Label-Low, Label-Lowest, and Label-Lowest 2.
